            DIRECTIVE:
            # Effort-breaker — `def:gr_transition`

## Target
`def:gr_transition` in `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_GrassmannianCells.tex`
(Lean target `AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap`).

## Why
This single declaration has been dispatched to a `mathlib-build` prover for TWO
consecutive iters and produced ZERO committed edits each time — no partial progress,
no decomposition handoff. It is the Cramer-inverse transition ring homomorphism on a
localized multivariate-polynomial ring, and as one monolithic `def` it is too large
for the prover to land in one go. The supporting node `def:gr_affine_chart`
(`affineChart`) is already done (`\leanok`), so this is a live frontier bottleneck.
Break it into a `\uses`-linked chain of small, independently-formalizable pieces so the
next prover iter has several ready atoms instead of one wall.

## Granularity
**Fine — one mathematical claim/construction per lemma**, split along the matrix-algebra
seams (not arbitrary points). After this break, no resulting block should require more
than a single matrix-algebra fact or a single localization-universal-property step.

## Proof structure to cut along
Work over the affine chart ring `R^I_J := ℤ[X^I, 1/P^I_J]` (the localization of the
chart polynomial ring `ℤ[X^I]` — i.e. `MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q // q ∉ I}) ℤ` — at the
minor determinant `P^I_J`). The construction of `θ_{I,J} : ℤ[X^J, 1/P^J_I] → R^I_J` in
the current proof sketch decomposes naturally as:

1. **The universal matrix and its `J`-minor over the localization.** `X^I` is the
   `d × r` matrix over `ℤ[X^I]` (identity block on the `I` columns, free indeterminates
   off `I`); base-changed into `R^I_J` it gives a matrix, and its `J`-minor
   `X^I_J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) R^I_J` (a `def`).
2. **The minor determinant is a unit in `R^I_J`.** `P^I_J = det (X^I_J)` is invertible
   in the localization `R^I_J` by construction of the chart (`IsUnit (det X^I_J)`). A
   `lem` — the load-bearing fact that lets Cramer's rule apply. [Mathlib:
   `IsLocalization.Away` makes the localized-at element a unit; `Matrix.isUnit_iff_isUnit_det`.]
3. **The Cramer inverse.** With the determinant a unit, `(X^I_J)⁻¹ :
   Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) R^I_J` exists with `(X^I_J)⁻¹ * X^I_J = 1` and
   `X^I_J * (X^I_J)⁻¹ = 1`. A `def` + a `lem` for the two inverse identities.
   [Mathlib: `Matrix.nonsing_inv`, `Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul`, `Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv`,
   gated on `IsUnit (det _)`.]
4. **The image matrix.** `M := (X^I_J)⁻¹ * X^I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I_J` — the
   matrix whose entries are the images of the `X^J` indeterminates. A `def`.
5. **The pre-localization ring hom.** The ℤ-algebra hom `ℤ[X^J] → R^I_J` sending each
   free indeterminate `x^J_{p,q}` to `M p q` (and the `J`-block to the identity). A
   `def` via `MvPolynomial` universal property (`MvPolynomial.aeval`/`eval₂`).
6. **The transition unit identity.** Under the pre-hom, `P^J_I` (the `I`-minor
   determinant of `X^J`) maps to `det ((X^I_J)⁻¹ X^I_I) = det((X^I_J)⁻¹) = (P^I_J)⁻¹`,
   a unit in `R^I_J`. A `lem` — this is exactly what lets the hom extend to the source
   localization. [`RingHom.IsUnit`, `IsUnit.map`.]
7. **The localization extension = `def:gr_transition`.** The pre-hom sends `P^J_I` to a
   unit, so it factors through `ℤ[X^J, 1/P^J_I] → R^I_J` — this is `transitionMap`. The
   final `def`, now a thin `IsLocalization.Away.lift` / `IsLocalization.lift` wrapper
   over steps 5–6. [Mathlib: `IsLocalization.Away.lift`, `IsLocalization.lift`.]

Keep `\theta_{I,I} = \id` as a one-line corollary (`lem`) of step 7, or fold it into the
existing `lem:gr_cocycle` deps — your call, but make sure `lem:gr_cocycle`'s `\uses{}`
points at whatever new sub-blocks it actually needs (it currently `\uses{def:gr_transition,
def:gr_affine_chart}`).

## Requirements
- Each new sub-block gets its own `\label`, `\lean{...}` (a concrete declaration name in
  the `AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian` namespace), a `% LEAN SIGNATURE` block, an
  informal proof, and an accurate `\uses{}` chain. Re-anchor genuine Mathlib facts
  (`Matrix.nonsing_inv` identities, `IsLocalization.Away.lift`) as `\mathlibok` Mathlib
  dependency anchors where that keeps the DAG honest — but `\mathlibok` ONLY on real
  Mathlib re-exports, never on the project's own to-be-built decls.
- Preserve the existing `% SOURCE` / `% SOURCE QUOTE` Nitsure §1 citations; distribute
  the relevant fragment to whichever sub-block it now justifies.
- Do NOT add `\leanok` to anything (the deterministic sync owns it).
- After the break, `def:gr_transition` itself must remain the named target
  `transitionMap` (step 7), so downstream `\uses{def:gr_transition}` edges stay valid.
- Keep `lem:gr_cocycle`, `def:gr_glued_scheme`, etc. intact; only repoint their `\uses{}`
  if the dependency they need is now a new sub-block.

32	
33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.
38	
39	## Directive Format
40	
41	```markdown
42	# Effort Breaker Directive
43	
44	## Slug
45	<slug>
46	
47	## Target
48	<the blueprint label to break, e.g. thm:flattening_stratification_exists>
49	
50	## Granularity
51	<"one level" (the proof's main steps) | "fine — one mathematical claim per
52	 lemma" (when a coarser break already happened and a piece is still hard)>
53	
54	## Proof structure
55	<the seams to cut along: the steps of the target's informal proof, or the
56	 structure of the source proof. The dispatcher gives you this so you split at
57	 real mathematical boundaries.>
58	
59	## Strategy context
60	<the slice of STRATEGY.md that matters — what this result supports. You do NOT
61	 read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
62	
63	## References
64	- <path/to/reference.md>: <which proof / steps live here>
65	```
66	
67	## How you break
68	
69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).
70	2. Read the target's informal proof (and the source proof it cites, per the directive's references). Identify the **steps** — each self-contained claim the proof establishes on the way to the conclusion.
71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),
73	   - the **statement** of that step in the project's notation,
74	   - an informal `\begin{proof}` for the step with `\uses{...}` naming its own dependencies (existing blocks, Mathlib anchors, or earlier sub-lemmas in this chain),
75	   - citation discipline (below) when the step derives from a source.
76	4. **Rewrite the target's proof** to invoke the chain: a short proof body that combines `L1 … L_n`, with `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}` (plus any direct deps that remain). The target keeps its statement and `\lean{}` unchanged.
77	5. Each `L_i` must be **strictly smaller** than the target (smaller proof, fewer moving parts). If a step is still large, either break it into sub-steps now (at fine granularity) or flag it in the report so the dispatcher can re-break it next round.
78	
79	Granularity is the directive's call. "One level" = the proof's main steps. "Fine" = one mathematical assertion per lemma — the limit is the same as the fine-grained prover: a lemma so small its proof is a single move. There is no floor; if a break didn't help, the next dispatch breaks further.
80	
81	## Markers and citation (same rules as every blueprint agent)
82	
83	- **Never add `\leanok`** — it is the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase's job.
84	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor** — when a step is a result Mathlib already provides, write it as an anchor (`\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}`, `\mathlibok`, `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`, no proof) rather than re-proving it. Mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote — a wrong anchor is worse than leaving the step to prove.
85	- **Citation discipline (hard rule):** every block derived from a source needs `% SOURCE:` (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a verbatim original-language `% SOURCE QUOTE:` copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` first line. Never write these from memory; if you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait, read the new file, then write the cited block. Never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
86	
87	## Rules
88	
89	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). No `.lean`, no `content.tex`, no `macros/`. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
90	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces).
91	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no project-history narrative.
92	- **Preserve the target's statement and `\lean{}`.** You re-express its *proof* as a chain; you do not weaken or restate the theorem. (If the target's statement looks wrong, do NOT silently fix it — flag it under "Notes" as a strategy item.)
93	- **Conserve the mathematics.** The chain must actually prove the target — every gap the original proof crossed is covered by some `L_i`. Don't drop a hard step by relabelling it; a step you cannot decompose or prove is reported, not hidden.
94	- **Verify with the graph.** After breaking, run `archon dag-query node --node <target>` and `archon dag-query ancestors --node <target>`: the target's effort should drop, the new `L_i` should appear, and there must be no broken `\uses{}`. Fix and re-check.
95	
96	## Workflow
97	
98	1. Read the directive and the target block on disk.
99	2. Read the references the directive names; identify the proof's steps.
100	3. Author the sub-lemma chain (statement + proof + `\uses` for each), bottom-up so later lemmas can `\uses{}` earlier ones.
101	4. Rewrite the target's proof to invoke the chain.
102	5. For any step needing a source you lack, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
103	6. Re-query the graph; confirm the target's effort dropped and no `\uses{}` broke.
104	7. Write your report.
105	
106	## Logging
107	
108	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/effort-breaker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
109	
110	```markdown

115	
116	## Target
117	<label>
118	
119	## Status
120	<COMPLETE — target re-expressed as a chain | PARTIAL — a step could not be broken/proved (listed below)>
121	
122	## Effort before → after
123	- target `effort_local`: <before> → <after>
124	- sub-lemmas added: <count>
125	
126	## Chain added (target ← L_n ← … ← L1)
127	- `\label{lem:L1}` `\lean{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <statement, one line> (effort ≈ <small>)
128	- `\label{lem:L2}` … — <statement> (\uses{lem:L1})
129	- ...
130	- Target `<label>` proof rewritten: `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}`.
131	
132	## Still hard (re-break candidates)
133	- `lem:Lk` — still large; re-dispatch the breaker at finer granularity, OR <why it's irreducible>.
134	
135	## Could not decompose (strategy items)
136	- <step> — <why: genuinely atomic / novel / statement looks wrong>.
137	
138	## References consulted
139	- `references/<file>.md` — <verbatim content taken>.
140	
141	## Notes for dispatcher
142	- `\lean{}` names I assigned by convention (confirm/scaffold): <list>.
143	- needed macros / cross-chapter concerns.
144	```
145	
146	## Return Value
147	
148	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	% NOTE: no `% archon:covers` line — these GR-cells/GR-glue declarations are destined
4	% for their own Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianCells.lean (1:1 slug
5	% mapping), a deliberate split from QuotScheme.lean for parallel proving once QUOT-repr
6	% activates. QuotScheme.lean is covered solely by Picard_QuotScheme.tex.
7	
8	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter blueprints the FIRST TWO sub-phases (GR-cells,
9	% GR-glue) of the Grassmannian-scheme construction that ultimately underlies
10	% thm:grassmannian_representable in chapter Picard_QuotScheme. Following
11	% [Nitsure], \S 1 ("Construction of Grassmannian"), the scheme
12	% \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) is built directly by gluing
13	% \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
14	% along explicit matrix-algebra transition maps, and shown smooth, separated
15	% and proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\). The later sub-phases (GR-quot: tautological
16	% quotient; GR-repr: functor-of-points; and the relative-over-\(S\) version
17	% with a vector bundle \(V\)) are OUT OF SCOPE here and are blueprinted with
18	% def:grassmannian_scheme / thm:grassmannian_representable in
19	% chapter Picard_QuotScheme. The declarations below are deliberately disjoint
20	% from that chapter's: this chapter adds only the construction lemmas for the
21	% absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\).
22	
23	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
24	\label{sec:gr_setup}
25	
26	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
27	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
28	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
29	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
30	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
31	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
32	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
33	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
34	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
35	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
36	
37	\begin{definition}
38	
39	\leanok
40	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
41	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
43	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
44	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
45	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
48	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
49	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
50	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
51	  %  $I$.
52	  %
53	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
54	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
55	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
56	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
57	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
58	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
59	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
60	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
61	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
62	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
63	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
64	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
65	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
66	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
67	  set
68	  \[
69	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
70	  \]
71	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
72	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
73	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
74	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
75	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
76	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
77	\end{definition}
78	
79	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
80	\label{sec:gr_transition}
81	
82	\begin{definition}
83	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
84	  \label{def:gr_transition}
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
86	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
87	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
88	  % affine patches" (read from
89	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
90	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
91	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
92	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
93	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
94	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
95	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
96	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
97	  %  on $U^I_J$.
98	  %
99	  %  For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
100	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
101	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
102	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
103	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
104	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
105	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
106	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
107	  For \(I, J\) of cardinality \(d\), set
108	  \(P^I_J := \det(X^I_J) \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the determinant of the \(J\)-th
109	  minor of \(X^I\), and let
110	  \[
111	    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]
112	  \]
113	  be the principal open subscheme of \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) on
114	  which \(P^I_J\) is invertible; over \(U^I_J\) the matrix \(X^I_J\) is
115	  invertible, with inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) whose entries lie in
116	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) by Cramer's rule. Note \(P^I_I = 1\), so
117	  \(U^I_I = U^I\). The \emph{transition map} \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the ring
118	  homomorphism
119	  \[
120	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
121	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
122	    \qquad
123	    \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I,
124	  \]
125	  i.e.\ the indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) maps to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the
126	  \(d \times r\) matrix \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This is well defined: the
127	  \(J\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}\),
128	  matching the identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\), and the \(I\)-minor of
129	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), so
130	  \[
131	    \theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J
132	  \]
133	  is invertible in the target; hence \(\theta_{I,J}\) extends from
134	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) to the localisation \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\). Dually,
135	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
136	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\), which we also denote \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the
137	  matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). One has \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\).
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\begin{lemma}
141	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
142	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
144	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
145	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
146	  % affine patches" (read from
147	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
148	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
149	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
150	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
151	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
152	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
153	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
154	  %  is satisfied."
155	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
156	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
157	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
158	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
159	  \[
160	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
161	  \]
162	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
163	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
164	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
165	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
166	  glue the charts.
167	\end{lemma}
168	
169	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
170	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
171	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
172	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
173	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
174	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
175	% L838-L848.)
176	\begin{proof}
177	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
178	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
179	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
180	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
181	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
182	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
183	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
184	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
185	
186	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
187	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
188	  \[
189	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
190	  \]
191	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
192	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
193	  \[
194	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
195	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
196	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
197	  \]
198	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
199	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
200	  \[
201	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
202	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
203	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
204	  \]
205	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
206	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
207	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
208	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
209	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
210	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
211	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
212	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
213	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
214	\end{proof}
215	
216	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
217	\label{sec:gr_glued}
218	
219	\begin{definition}
220	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
221	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
222	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
223	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
224	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
225	  % affine patches" (read from
226	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
227	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
228	  % "Therefore the schemes
229	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
230	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
231	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
232	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
233	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
234	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
235	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
236	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
237	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
238	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
239	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
240	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
241	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
242	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
243	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
244	  \[
245	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
246	  \]
247	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
248	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
249	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
250	
251	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
252	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
253	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
254	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
255	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
256	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
257	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
258	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
259	  follows.
260	\end{definition}
261	
262	\section{Separatedness}
263	\label{sec:gr_separated}
264	
265	\begin{lemma}
266	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
267	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
268	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
269	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
270	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
271	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

292	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
293	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
294	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
295	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
296	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
297	  homomorphism is surjective.

300	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
301	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
302	  \[

304	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
305	    \qquad

307	  \]

309	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
310	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
311	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
312	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
313	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
314	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
315	  and in particular

396	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
397	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
398	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
399	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
400	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
401	  \[
402	    \begin{array}{ccc}
403	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
404	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
405	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
406	    \end{array}
407	  \]
408	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
409	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
410	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
411	
412	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
413	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
414	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
415	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
416	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
417	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
418	  \(K\). Define
419	  \[
420	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
421	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
422	  \]

424	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
425	  landing in \(U^J\).

428	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
429	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
430	  \[

432	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},

434	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
435	  \]

439	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
440	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
441	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
442	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore

444	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
445	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)

456	\section{Out of scope}
457	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}

459	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
460	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
461	and properness -- the GR-cells and GR-glue sub-phases. The following are

464	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
465	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
466	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding (GR-quot).
467	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
468	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
469	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
470	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\) (GR-repr); this is blueprinted as
471	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
472	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
473	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
474	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
475	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
476	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
477	    that base change.
478	\end{itemize}

\begin{definition}
[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
  \label{def:gr_transition}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
  %  on $U^I_J$.
  %
  %  For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  For \(I, J\) of cardinality \(d\), set
  \(P^I_J := \det(X^I_J) \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the determinant of the \(J\)-th
  minor of \(X^I\), and let
  \[
    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]
  \]
  be the principal open subscheme of \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) on
  which \(P^I_J\) is invertible; over \(U^I_J\) the matrix \(X^I_J\) is
  invertible, with inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) whose entries lie in
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) by Cramer's rule. Note \(P^I_I = 1\), so
  \(U^I_I = U^I\). The \emph{transition map} \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the ring
  homomorphism
  \[
    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
    \qquad
    \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I,
  \]
  i.e.\ the indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) maps to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the
  \(d \times r\) matrix \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This is well defined: the
  \(J\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}\),
  matching the identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\), and the \(I\)-minor of
  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), so
  \[
    \theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J
  \]
  is invertible in the target; hence \(\theta_{I,J}\) extends from
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) to the localisation \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\). Dually,
  \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\), which we also denote \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the
  matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). One has \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\).
\end{definition}

% NOTE: The transition map def:gr_transition was decomposed (effort-breaker,
% iter-011) into the matrix-algebra chain below. The original Nitsure §1
% SOURCE QUOTE (L822-L836) is distributed across the sub-blocks that each
% fragment now justifies. The named Lean target transitionMap remains
% def:gr_transition (the final localization-lift, step 7); downstream
% \uses{def:gr_transition} edges are unchanged.

\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}

The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
anchors.

\begin{lemma}
[Localised element is a unit]
  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
  away from \(x\) (\(S = R[1/x]\), i.e.\ \([\operatorname{IsLocalization.Away} x\,
  S]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the structure map
  \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[A square matrix is invertible iff its determinant is]
  \label{lem:mathlib_isUnit_iff_isUnit_det}
  \lean{Matrix.isUnit_iff_isUnit_det}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring, \(A\) is a unit of the
  matrix ring iff \(\det A\) is a unit of the scalar ring.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[Left Cramer inverse]
  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[Right Cramer inverse]
  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[Universal property of an away-localisation]
  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
\end{lemma}

\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}

We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
ambient chart ring is \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{1,\dots,d\}
\times (\{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I), \mathbb{Z})\) of \cref{def:gr_affine_chart}.

\begin{definition}
[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
  with identity \(I\)-minor.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). `minorDet d r I J : R^I` is the
  %   determinant of the `d × d` submatrix of `universalMatrix d r I` whose
  %   columns are indexed by `J`, reindexed to `Fin d` via the order
  %   isomorphism `Fin d ≃o J` coming from `#J = d`:
  %   `(universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J) |>.det`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
  \[
    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
  \]
  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
  %   `universalMinor d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
  %   is the `J`-minor of `universalMatrix d r I` base-changed along the
  %   structure map `R^I → R^I_J = Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`:
  %   `((universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J)).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
  %  on $U^I_J$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
  %   `isUnit_det_universalMinor d r I J : IsUnit (universalMinor d r I J).det`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
  \[
    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
  \]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}
[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
  %  on $U^I_J$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
  %   `:= (universalMinor d r I J)⁻¹` (the Mathlib nonsingular inverse).
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). A conjunction (or pair of
  %   lemmas):
  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J * universalMinor d r I J = 1` and
  %   `universalMinor d r I J * universalMinorInv d r I J = 1`.
  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
  genuine two-sided inverse:
  \[
    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
  \]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}
[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "The images of the variables
  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
  %   `imageMatrix d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
  %   `:= universalMinorInv d r I J * (universalMatrix d r I).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Define the \(d \times r\) matrix over \(R^I_J\)
  \[
    M \;:=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I \;\in\; \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(R^I_J),
  \]
  the product of the Cramer inverse (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with the
  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to
  \(R^I_J\). Its entries are the prospective images of the indeterminates
  \(x^J_{p,q}\) under \(\theta_{I,J}\). Its \(J\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J =
  1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so \(M\) is a
  point of the chart \(U^J\); and its \(I\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I =
  (X^I_J)^{-1}\).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\)]
  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap}
  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "a ring homomorphism
  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Via the universal property of the
  %   polynomial chart ring `R^J := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ J}) ℤ`,
  %   `transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →ₐ[ℤ] Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
  %   `:= MvPolynomial.aeval (fun e => imageMatrix d r I J e.1 e.2.1)`, i.e. the
  %   free indeterminate `x^J_{p,q}` is sent to the `(p,q)`-entry of `imageMatrix`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
  \[
    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
  \]
  which is a unit.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}
[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
  \label{def:gr_transition}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
  %  defined as follows. ...
  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
  \[
    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
  \]
  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
\end{proof}
